{{Short description|Substance of which physical objects are composed}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Matter
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Atom]], [[Mass]], [[Energy]], [[Quantum mechanics]], [[Particle physics]]
}}

In [[physics]], '''matter''' is any substance that has [[mass]] and occupies [[volume|space]] by having [[volume]]. All everyday objects that can be touched are ultimately composed of [[atom]]s, which are themselves composed of interacting [[subatomic particle]]s. In both classical and modern physics, matter includes any entity that possesses [[mass]] (including [[rest mass]]) and volume.

== Definition ==

=== Based on atoms ===
Historically, matter was defined as anything composed of [[atom]]s — the smallest units of [[chemical element]]s that retain chemical properties.

=== Based on mass and volume ===
A common definition: matter is anything that has mass and occupies space (has volume).

=== Based on quarks and leptons ===
In [[particle physics]], ordinary matter is composed of [[fermion]]s — specifically [[quark]]s and [[lepton]]s — held together by [[fundamental interaction]]s.

== States of matter ==
{{Main|State of matter}}
Matter exists in several states (phases):
* '''[[Solid]]''' — fixed shape and volume; atoms closely packed
* '''[[Liquid]]''' — fixed volume, variable shape; atoms close but mobile
* '''[[Gas]]''' — variable shape and volume; atoms far apart, free movement
* '''[[Plasma (physics)|Plasma]]''' — ionised gas; most common state in the [[universe]]
* '''[[Bose–Einstein condensate]]''' — ultra-cold quantum state of bosons
* '''[[Fermionic condensate]]''' — ultra-cold quantum state of fermions

== Matter vs. energy ==
[[Mass–energy equivalence]] (<math>E = mc^2</math>) blurs the classical distinction between matter and [[energy]]. Matter can be converted to energy in [[nuclear reaction]]s, and energy can create matter ([[pair production]]).

== Dark matter ==
{{Main|Dark matter}}
Approximately 85% of the matter in the universe is [[dark matter]] — matter that does not interact with [[electromagnetic radiation]] and is detected only through its gravitational effects. Its composition remains one of the greatest unsolved problems in physics.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Antimatter]]
* [[Atom]]
* [[Dark matter]]
* [[Mass]]
* [[Mass–energy equivalence]]
* [[State of matter]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Matter| ]]
[[Category:Concepts in physics]]
[[Category:Physical sciences]]
